era_context: The early 18th century, dominated by the aftermath of the War of Spanish Succession and the emergence of new European power balances. The Russian Empire under Peter the Great was consolidating territorial gains and modernizing institutions. The Qing Dynasty in China was at its height under the Kangxi Emperor's reign (to 1722) and his successor Yongzheng (1722–1735). The Ottoman Empire remained a major Mediterranean and Middle Eastern power, though beginning a long relative decline. European colonial expansion in the Americas, Africa, and Asia was accelerating.

events.title: Death of Peter the Great; Catherine I assumes Russian throne
events.description: Peter the Great died on 28 January 1725 (8 February 1726 in the Gregorian calendar used here). His widow, Catherine I, assumed the throne with the backing of the military and nobility, particularly through the influence of Alexander Menshikov. This marked a significant transition in Russian governance, as Catherine I was the first woman to rule Russia in her own right. Her reign (1725–1727) was characterized by the continued consolidation of Peter's reforms and the rise of court factionalism. The succession was contested and unstable, setting the stage for decades of palace intrigue.
key_figures: Alexander Menshikov (court favourite and power broker)

sources.name: Contemporary court records and diplomatic correspondence from European ambassadors in St. Petersburg
Polnoe sobranie zakonov Rossiiskoi imperii (Complete Collection of Laws of the Russian Empire)

events.certainty_note: The date of Peter's death and Catherine's accession are well-documented in contemporary diplomatic records and Russian state archives. The Gregorian calendar date is certain.
cross_references: 1725-001 (Peter the Great's death in Julian calendar)

events.title: Yongzheng Emperor consolidates power in Qing China; purges rivals
events.description: The Yongzheng Emperor (r. 1722–1735), who had assumed the throne in 1722 following his father Kangxi's death, continued aggressive consolidation of power in 1726. He purged rivals and potential challengers, including his brothers and court officials suspected of disloyalty. The Yongzheng Emperor implemented administrative reforms, including the creation of the Grand Council (Junjichu) to centralize decision-making and reduce the power of the traditional imperial bureaucracy. These measures strengthened imperial authority but also created resentment among the literati and nobility. His reign marked a shift toward more autocratic governance within the Confucian framework.

sources.name: Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty (Qing Shilu)
Court memorials and edicts from the Yongzheng period

events.certainty_note: The Yongzheng Emperor's consolidation of power and administrative reforms are well-documented in Qing state records and later historiography. Dating is certain.

events.title: Treaty of Wusterhausen signed between Prussia and Russia
events.description: On 12 October 1726, Prussia and Russia signed the Treaty of Wusterhausen (also called the Treaty of Berlin), a secret agreement that aligned the two powers against Austria and other rivals. The treaty was negotiated by Frederick William I of Prussia and Russian diplomats, and it represented an early step in the diplomatic realignment that would characterize mid-18th-century European politics. The agreement included mutual defense provisions and was part of a broader strategy by both powers to counter Austrian Habsburg dominance. This treaty is often seen as a precursor to the later Russo-Prussian alliance that would emerge under Frederick the Great.

sources.name: Diplomatic correspondence and treaty texts in Prussian and Russian state archives
Recueil des traités de paix, d'alliance, de commerce, etc. (Collection of Treaties)

events.certainty_note: The treaty is well-documented in diplomatic archives and contemporary records. The date and terms are certain.

events.title: Jonathan Swift publishes Gulliver's Travels
events.region: Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland
events.description: Jonathan Swift's satirical novel Gulliver's Travels was published in October 1726 in London. The work, presented as a travel narrative, contained sharp political and social satire directed at contemporary British politics, the Royal Society, and human nature itself. The novel was an immediate success and became one of the most influential works of English literature. Its four voyages—to Lilliput, Brobdingnag, Laputa, and the land of the Houyhnhnms—allowed Swift to critique everything from political factionalism to scientific pretension. The work's enduring popularity reflects both its literary merit and the universality of its satirical targets.
events.certainty_note: The publication date and details are well-documented in publishing records and contemporary literary sources.

events.title: Vitus Bering leads Second Kamchatka Expedition; explores Bering Strait
events.region: Russian Far East and North Pacific
events.description: Vitus Bering, a Danish navigator in Russian service, departed from St. Petersburg in 1725 to lead the Second Kamchatka Expedition (also called the Great Northern Expedition). In 1726, Bering was actively exploring the waters between Kamchatka and Alaska, seeking to determine whether Asia and North America were connected by land. His expedition would eventually establish that they were separated by water (the Bering Strait), though Bering himself did not sight the American coast. The expedition lasted until 1730 and produced the first detailed maps of the Russian Far East and the northern Pacific. This exploration was crucial for Russian claims to Alaska and the Pacific Northwest.

sources.name: Bering's expedition journals and reports to the Russian Academy of Sciences

events.certainty_note: The expedition's timeline and Bering's activities in 1726 are well-documented in Russian archives and expedition records.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the Treaty of Wusterhausen (1726-003) has been debated by historians. Some scholars argue that it was less consequential than once believed, as both Prussia and Russia continued to pursue independent foreign policies and the alliance was not consistently honored. The treaty is sometimes overstated in older historiography as a turning point in European diplomacy, when in fact the real realignment occurred later in the 1730s and 1740s. Additionally, the extent of Menshikov's influence over Catherine I (1726-001) has been reassessed; some modern scholars suggest that Catherine I exercised more independent authority than earlier accounts suggested, though the broad outlines of his influence remain accepted.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1726 is rich, particularly for European and East Asian events. European diplomatic and court records are abundant, and Chinese imperial records are comprehensive. However, the record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military history; economic, social, and cultural history of non-elite populations is underrepresented. Indigenous perspectives on European exploration (1726-005) are almost entirely absent from the surviving record. The Russian sources are generally reliable but reflect the perspective of the centralizing state. Chinese sources are official and may downplay internal dissent or administrative failures.

graph_edges.note: Catherine I's reign set the stage for the succession crisis that followed her death in 1727
Yongzheng's consolidation of power in 1726 was part of his broader reign of administrative reform (1722–1735)
The Wusterhausen alignment contributed to the diplomatic configurations of the War of Austrian Succession
Swift's satire on political factionalism was contemporary with the Russian succession crisis
Bering's 1726 explorations were part of the larger Second Kamchatka Expedition (1725–1730)
